rights.basis: The edition was published in 1909 and is public domain in the United States by publication date.

entities.label: Arnold Hahn's working copies of the C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I witnesses
notes: Bälz explicitly credits Hahn with copies of C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I and excludes B from that statement.
The current schema has no working-transcript entity type; manuscript_witness is used provisionally for this material intermediary, not to claim medieval date or independent textual authority.
The copies' present location and survival have not been established.

next_steps: Request a complete scan through a holding library or an authenticated Google Books/HathiTrust workflow.
Retain the cached 71-page title-and-edition selection as a complete local copy only of its stated selection boundary, not of the whole dissertation.
Do not bypass CAPTCHA or provider verification controls.
openQuestions.question: Can a stable, complete copy of Bälz's 1909 dissertation, including all thirty-two preliminary pages, be acquired through a provider route that can be recorded without defeating an automated verification challenge?

next_steps: Search Bälz and Hahn archival papers and correspondence for the copies and collation notes.
Compare Bälz's B apparatus against Horstmann 1874 and Bodley 779.
Keep Horstmann 1874 as the supported B intermediary and Hahn's copies as the supported C/A/L/R/G/O/T/I intermediaries until direct consultation is evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Do Arnold Hahn's manuscript copies survive, what were their precise production and transmission histories, and did Bälz consult Bodley 779 directly rather than through Horstmann's 1874 edition?

next_steps: Compare distinctive readings against the Latin Navigatio recensions and the French verse tradition represented by Jubinal.
Consult scholarship after Lavery 1984 for a revised source model.
Keep Bälz's and Lavery's arguments as conflicting scholarly hypotheses rather than converting either into an identified physical source.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin Navigatio branch or exemplar underlies the poem, and how should Bälz's tentative Anglo-Norman source proposal be reconciled with Lavery's rejection of direct dependence on Benedeit's Voyage?

next_steps: Request the relevant leaves of Add. C. 38, Laud Misc. 463, Tanner 17, Trinity Oxford MS 57, Pepys MS 2344, and the four British Library witnesses through their repositories.
Request Lambeth MS 223 ff. 113r-122v, the reverse of Ripon Fragment 33, and Wisbech MS 21 through their mediated imaging routes.
Record supplied-image licenses and terms before redistribution.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public or permission-cleared image coverage be obtained for the Brendan loci not covered by the currently available digital selections?

next_steps: Collate lineation in Bälz, the source editions, and representative manuscripts.
Record whether rubrics, Amen, split lines, omissions, and witness-specific additions are counted.
Keep the 738 numbered lines in CME00141 separate from abstract-work, witness, and alternate-edition counts.
openQuestions.question: How should Bälz's and DIMEV's 738-line extent, Lavery's 734-line extent, PCMEP's 732 lines plus final Amen, and the 733-line CME00107 XML count be reconciled?

lineage.summary: CME00141 is the University of Michigan encoding of the title page and printed pp. 1-70 of Martha Bälz's 1909 critical edition of the Middle English Life of Saint Brendan. Bälz used Ashmole 43 (M) as her base text, normally retaining it and emending by stated criteria, and compared eleven further witnesses through a mixture of earlier printed editions and copies made by Arnold Hahn. DIMEV's five additional witnesses enlarge the modern census but are not retroactive sources of Bälz's edition. Complete public manuscript surrogates and every identified public selection object were cached where available; unpublicized surfaces and provider-controlled reproductions remain explicitly requestable or unresolved.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
catalogSummary.date: Composed 1250-1290, with circa 1260 suggested; extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; Bälz edition published 1909
catalogSummary.region: England; the original dialect is only broadly southern and no precise place of composition is established
catalogSummary.title: Life of Saint Brendan

catalogingSubject.label: Life of Saint Brendan
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the anonymous Middle English verse Life of Saint Brendan transmitted within the South English Legendary. CME00141 represents Martha Bälz's 1909 twelve-witness critical edition: an Ashmole 43 running base text, normally reached through Horstmann 1874, with readings from eleven comparison witnesses reached through printed editions, Arnold Hahn's manuscript copies, and one unresolved Bodley 779 route. Bälz's German title page and embedded critical apparatus are modeled as editorial content, not as a second medieval work. The record does not treat the whole South English Legendary, all seventeen DIMEV witnesses, the dissertation's omitted introduction, or any physical codex as if it were the work itself.

contentParts.label: Life of Saint Brendan

locators.value: running poem and apparatus on pp. 1-70

notes: The 738 count is the Bälz-edition XML stream. It is not silently reconciled with Lavery's 734, PCMEP's 732 plus Amen, or CME00107's 733 main lines.
The eleven comparison-witness readings are apparatus for this work, not additional content works.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous Middle English verse saints' life, represented by Bälz's critically edited Ashmole-based running text.

contentParts.label: Bälz's title-page framing and critical apparatus

locators.value: unnumbered title page and apparatus on printed pp. 1-70

notes: The 771 NOTE elements are textual apparatus entries, not 771 independent works or sections.
The dissertation's xxxii-page German introduction is outside CME00141 and outside the cached selection.
contentParts.summary: German thesis framing and 771 embedded critical notes recording readings from the M/H/W/B/C/A/L/R/G/O/T/I witness set.

assertion.notes: A specialist composition range for the abstract poem; it is not inferred from the date of any surviving witness.

Laud Misc. 108's target belongs to an early core within a codex whose broader history extends later, while Harley MS 2277 is dated circa 1300 (1295-1305). Ashmole 43 and Egerton 2891 also overlap the opening of the fourteenth century. The evidence does not establish which surviving witness is earliest, so no singular lineage entity is asserted. Bälz's 1909 date remains on the edition layer; it is not the work's first publication because earlier witness editions appeared in 1844 and 1874.

dateStatements.display: Extant by circa 1300 in overlapping early witnesses, including Laud Misc. 108 and Harley MS 2277

scope.description: Joint qualified attestation window among the seventeen witnesses researched

extent.description: The cataloged unit contains one medieval verse work plus editorial framing and apparatus. The XML has two DIV1 elements, no DIV2 or HEAD, one LG, 738 L, 771 NOTE, 71 PB, no MILESTONE, five P, 7,459 HI, one revision ITEM, and no ITEM1. The revision ITEM is header metadata, not work content. Bälz and DIMEV report 738 lines; Lavery reports 734, PCMEP reports 732 plus final Amen, and CME00107 has 733 main XML lines. Those measures remain explicitly unresolved.

formStatements.description: A Middle English poem in rhyming aa couplets, commonly described as septenary with seven stresses and two half-lines.

scope.description: Work-level form; the separately modeled editorial apparatus does not make the medieval work mixed

formStatements.description: German prose framing and critical notation containing Middle English witness readings.

assertion.notes: A descriptive narrative mode within the saints' life; not used here to reclassify the poem as secular romance.
Lavery emphasizes the plain vernacular didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary adaptation.

notes: The preferred title is a modern supplied work title; Bälz's German dissertation title and edited incipit remain separately identified.
Saint Brendan is the subject, not an attributed author. The medieval author or adapter is anonymous.
German belongs to Bälz's editorial framing and apparatus and is not exposed in the catalog summary as an intrinsic language of the medieval work.
The main work is verse. The mixed editorial-apparatus part does not change the work-level form to mixed.
Romance is not assigned as a primary genre: the voyage contains adventure and marvels, but the strongest grounded classification is hagiographic saints' legend and religious poetry.
Bälz's twelve-source edition is distinguished from DIMEV's seventeen-witness census; the five later collateral witnesses are related sources, not retroactive sources of the 1909 edition.
CME00141 and CME00107 represent the same abstract poem through different scholarly editions and retain their distinct edited lineation and apparatus boundaries.

The record remains partial because line-count conventions, source relationships, original linguistic localization, the Hahn/Bodley consultation routes, and access to Bälz's complete preliminaries remain unresolved.

next_steps: Collate lineation in Bälz, Horstmann, representative manuscripts, and a current critical edition.
Record separately whether Amen, rubrics, split lines, lacunae, and witness-specific additions are counted.
Retain 738 only as the Bälz/CME00141 line count until reconciled.
openQuestions.question: How should Bälz's and DIMEV's 738 lines, Lavery's 734 lines, PCMEP's 732 lines plus final Amen, and CME00107's 733 main XML lines be reconciled across witnesses and editorial conventions?

next_steps: Consult scholarship published after Lavery's 1984 study.
Compare distinctive narrative and verbal readings against Latin and Anglo-Norman textual traditions.
Keep work-level hypotheses separate from any claim about an identified physical exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin Navigatio recension or exemplar most directly underlies the poem, and how should Bälz's tentative French-source proposal be reconciled with Lavery's rejection of direct dependence on Benedeit's Voyage?

next_steps: Consult current linguistic and South English Legendary scholarship on reconstructed archetypal language.
Separate original-language claims from the local profiles of individual witnesses.
Retain England as broad cultural origin and southern England as tentative only.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's place and dialect of composition be localized more closely than broadly southern England without transferring properties from later witnesses or adopting Bälz's Gloucester argument uncritically?

next_steps: Search Bälz and Hahn archival papers and correspondence.
Compare Bälz's B apparatus with Horstmann 1874 and Bodley 779.
Retain the currently supported intermediary routes until direct consultation is evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Do Arnold Hahn's copy intermediaries survive, and did Bälz consult Bodley 779 directly rather than through Horstmann 1874?

next_steps: Request a complete scan through a holding library or authenticated provider workflow.
Retain the present local PDF as complete only for its title-page-and-pp. 1-70 selection boundary.
Recheck the preliminary source and method arguments against the complete dissertation when obtained.
openQuestions.question: Can a stable complete copy of Bälz 1909, including all thirty-two preliminary pages, be acquired without bypassing provider verification controls?

assertion.notes: The work is Middle English and part of the South English Legendary tradition; a more precise place of composition is not established.

PCMEP reports that the original dialect is unknown, though northern origin can be ruled out. Bälz's Gloucester framing belongs to an older scholarly argument and is not adopted as a firm composition place.

subjects.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

summaries.value: A Middle English verse life recounting Brendan's sacred sea voyage with a company of monks. The voyagers pass providential and marvelous islands, encounter the whale Jasconius, birds associated with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and paradisal spaces, and ultimately reach the Land of Promise before Brendan returns and dies. The narrative adapts hagiographic voyage material to the concise didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary.

CME00141 contains Bälz's German thesis title page and one 738-line Middle English poem in a single verse group, with 771 embedded critical notes. The XML has two DIV1 elements, one LG, 71 page breaks, five title-page paragraphs, and no subsidiary DIV2 sections. The cached source preserves the title page and printed pp. 1-70, the complete boundary encoded by CME00141, but not the dissertation's xxxii-page introduction.

The running text is based on Bälz's M, Ashmole 43, with eleven comparison witnesses. The supported routes are Horstmann 1874 for M; Horstmann 1887 for H; Wright 1844 for W; a probable Horstmann 1874 route for B, whose direct consultation remains unresolved; and Arnold Hahn's copies for C, A, L, R, G, O, T, and I. DIMEV lists five additional witnesses that belong to the wider tradition but are not sources of Bälz's edition.

Work, witness, edition, intermediary, facsimile, and encoding layers are kept separate. The thirteenth-century composition range belongs to the abstract poem; dates, regions, and dialects assigned to individual codices describe those witnesses; 1909 describes Bälz's publication; and the 738-line and 771-note counts describe CME00141's edited digital representation. Bälz's historical Gloucester localization is therefore not transferred to the abstract work as a confirmed place of composition.

assertion.notes: A supplied uniform work title, not a claim that every witness carries this exact heading.

subjects.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

work.summary: A Middle English verse life recounting Brendan's sacred sea voyage with a company of monks. The voyagers pass providential and marvelous islands, encounter the whale Jasconius, birds associated with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and paradisal spaces, and ultimately reach the Land of Promise before Brendan returns and dies. The narrative adapts hagiographic voyage material to the concise didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary.
